skill.name: firebase-crashlytics
skill.description: Guides Android and iOS developers through Firebase Crashlytics setup, verification, symbol upload, and report customization.
skill.summary: Set up Firebase Crashlytics for Android and iOS apps with setup, verification, and cleanup guidance.
skill.author: firebase

security_audit.summary: The static analyzer flagged many Markdown backticks, Firebase URLs, and product terms as high-risk code patterns, but no executable malware or prompt injection was found. The real residual risk is moderate: the skill documents network-installed Firebase CLI commands and temporary crash-test code that can mutate Firebase resources or break an app if used without cleanup.

medium_findings.title: Network-Installed Firebase CLI Commands
medium_findings.description: The skill documents `npx -y firebase-tools@latest` commands that can download tooling from npm and create or modify Firebase projects and apps. This is legitimate Firebase setup guidance, but users should review commands before execution and use the intended Firebase account and project.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The command examples are present and can perform network installs and Firebase resource changes. Confidence is not higher because the commands are transparent, user-directed, and aligned with the skill purpose.

medium_findings.title: Intentional Crash-Test Code Requires Cleanup
medium_findings.description: The setup guides instruct users to add code that deliberately crashes the app to verify Crashlytics. The guides also instruct removal after verification, so this is an operational risk rather than malicious behavior.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The intentional crash snippets are explicit and could cause denial of service if left in production. The surrounding cleanup guidance reduces the risk and supports a medium confidence rating.

low_findings.title: Markdown Backticks Misclassified as Code Execution
low_findings.description: Most external command findings are Markdown inline code, Gradle snippets, Swift snippets, or file names. There are no Ruby files, shell backtick expressions, or automatic execution hooks in the reviewed files.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged content is visible Markdown documentation, not executable Ruby or shell backtick code. The repository files reviewed are documentation files only.

low_findings.title: Firebase Documentation Links Are Benign
low_findings.description: The hardcoded URLs point to Firebase documentation, Google Maven, and the Firebase iOS SDK repository. They support setup instructions and do not send local data to external endpoints.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URLs are readable Firebase or Google-owned references used for dependency lookup and documentation. No evidence found of hidden network requests or exfiltration.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Findings Are False Positives
low_findings.description: The high-severity weak cryptography findings appear to match product and crash-reporting text, not cryptographic algorithms. No evidence found of MD5, SHA1, DES, RC4, or custom cryptographic code in the reviewed files.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines describe Crashlytics and native crash support, not cryptographic behavior. Confidence is high after reviewing all three files listed by the static analyzer.

low_findings.title: Project Inspection Is Setup Context
low_findings.description: System reconnaissance findings are ordinary setup steps such as checking existing Firebase config files, project Gradle files, and bundle identifiers. No evidence found that the skill collects secrets or exports discovered data.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The inspection guidance is necessary to configure Firebase apps and stays within local project setup. The reviewed files contain no persistence, credential harvesting, or exfiltration instructions.

dangerous_patterns.title: Use of `npx -y` with Latest Firebase Tools
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill recommends `npx -y firebase-tools@latest`, which auto-installs the latest Firebase CLI package. This is common for Firebase workflows, but it should be reviewed before execution in controlled environments.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The exact pattern exists and causes network package resolution. The risk is bounded because the package name is explicit and belongs to the Firebase CLI workflow.

content.value_statement: Mobile teams need reliable crash reporting without missing platform-specific setup details. This skill guides Crashlytics setup, verification, symbol upload, and report customization for Android and iOS apps.
actual_capabilities: Guides Firebase Crashlytics setup for Android Gradle projects.
Guides Firebase Crashlytics setup for iOS Swift Package Manager projects.
Explains Firebase project and app provisioning with Firebase CLI commands.
Describes Android native symbol and iOS dSYM upload requirements.
Provides temporary crash-test steps to verify report ingestion.
Links to Firebase documentation for custom keys, logs, user identifiers, and non-fatal reports.
limitations: It does not provision Firebase resources without user-approved command execution.
It does not inspect live Crashlytics data unless a Firebase MCP server is available.
It does not replace Firebase console access or Firebase account permissions.
Version placeholders require users to confirm current SDK and plugin versions.

use_cases.title: Add Crash Reporting to Android
use_cases.description: Use the guide to add the Crashlytics Gradle plugin, dependencies, config file, and verification crash to an Android app.

use_cases.title: Configure Crashlytics for iOS
use_cases.description: Use the guide to register an iOS app, add FirebaseCrashlytics, configure dSYM upload, and verify crash delivery.

use_cases.title: Standardize Mobile Release Diagnostics
use_cases.description: Use the guide to document setup checks, symbol upload requirements, and cleanup steps before mobile releases.

prompt_templates.title: Start Android Setup
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me set up Firebase Crashlytics in my Android app. Check what files you need first, then explain each change before making it.

prompt_templates.title: Start iOS Setup
prompt_templates.prompt: Guide me through adding Firebase Crashlytics to my iOS app. Include Firebase app registration, package setup, dSYM upload, and verification.

prompt_templates.title: Verify Existing Configuration
prompt_templates.prompt: Review my mobile project for Crashlytics setup gaps. Check dependencies, config files, symbol upload settings, and test crash cleanup.

prompt_templates.title: Plan Cross-Platform Rollout
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a rollout checklist for Crashlytics across Android and iOS. Include CLI provisioning, version checks, symbolication, privacy review, and release validation.

output_examples.input: Set up Crashlytics for an Android app that already has a Firebase project.
output: A short preflight checklist for the Firebase app and config file.
A list of Gradle plugin and dependency changes to review.
A verification plan that includes a temporary test crash and cleanup step.

output_examples.input: Check whether my iOS Crashlytics setup is complete.
output: A review of package dependency, Firebase initialization, and dSYM upload requirements.
A clear list of missing setup items and manual Xcode checks.
A final verification sequence for confirming the crash report appears.

output_examples.input: Prepare Crashlytics for a release candidate.
output: A release readiness checklist for Android and iOS symbolication.
A reminder to remove temporary crash code before release.
A set of recommended custom keys and logs to improve triage.

best_practices: Review Firebase CLI commands before execution and confirm the active Firebase account and project.
Remove all temporary crash-test code immediately after Crashlytics receives the verification report.
Confirm symbol upload configuration before release so native and iOS crashes are readable.
anti_patterns: Do not leave forced crash code in a branch that can ship to users.
Do not add user identifiers or custom keys without a privacy review.
Do not pin placeholder SDK versions without checking the current Firebase release guidance.

faq.question: Does this skill create Firebase projects automatically?
faq.answer: No. It documents Firebase CLI commands, but execution should require user review and approval.

faq.question: Can it help with both Android and iOS?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes separate guidance for Android Gradle projects and iOS Swift Package Manager projects.

faq.question: Does it include Crashlytics verification steps?
faq.answer: Yes. It explains how to trigger a temporary test crash and confirm the report appears in Firebase.

faq.question: Does it cover native Android crashes?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes optional guidance for the Crashlytics NDK dependency and native symbol upload.

faq.question: Does it cover iOS dSYM uploads?
faq.answer: Yes. It describes the required build setting and Crashlytics run script phase.

faq.question: Does it read Crashlytics reports?
faq.answer: Only when the Firebase MCP server is installed and the user has access to the Firebase project.
